questions.q: Bhaskara I is best remembered as a master of which two fields?
questions.explanation: Bhaskara I was a 7th-century Indian mathematician and astronomer.

questions.q: Bhaskara I is most famous for an accurate approximation formula for which function?
questions.explanation: He is celebrated for a remarkably accurate rational approximation of the sine function.

questions.q: In which century did Bhaskara I live and work?
questions.explanation: Bhaskara I lived in roughly 600 to 680 CE, the 7th century.

questions.q: Bhaskara I was a leading follower and expounder of the work of which earlier astronomer?
questions.explanation: Bhaskara I was the most important member of the school of Aryabhata and championed his ideas.

questions.q: Bhaskara I is often credited as the first to write numbers in which numeral system?
questions.explanation: His 629 CE work is the earliest known text writing numbers in the Hindu decimal place-value system.

questions.q: What symbol did Bhaskara I use to represent zero in his place-value notation?
questions.explanation: He used a small circle to denote zero in the positional decimal system.

questions.q: To avoid confusion with the later Bhaskara II, Bhaskara I is sometimes distinguished by adding which qualifier?
questions.explanation: He is called Bhaskara the Elder or specified as the 7th-century Bhaskara to distinguish him from the 12th-century Bhaskara II.

questions.q: Bhaskara I and the other classical Indian astronomers belonged to which broad tradition of astronomy?
questions.explanation: He worked within the Siddhanta tradition of mathematical astronomy in ancient India.

questions.q: Bhaskara I's surviving works are written primarily in which language?
questions.explanation: Like other classical Indian mathematical texts, his works were composed in Sanskrit.

questions.q: Bhaskara I's sine approximation lets one calculate sine values without needing what?
questions.explanation: His simple rational formula gives close sine values directly, avoiding the need for extensive tables.

questions.q: In 629 CE, Bhaskara I wrote an influential commentary on which foundational text?
questions.explanation: His Aryabhatiyabhasya of 629 CE is a detailed commentary on Aryabhata's Aryabhatiya.

questions.q: Which of these is one of Bhaskara I's two major astronomical treatises?
questions.explanation: The Mahabhaskariya (Greater Book of Bhaskara) is one of his principal astronomical works.

questions.q: Besides the Mahabhaskariya, which shorter astronomical work is also attributed to Bhaskara I?
questions.explanation: The Laghubhaskariya (Smaller Book of Bhaskara) is his abridged companion treatise.

questions.q: Bhaskara I's sine approximation is a ratio of two polynomials, that is, what kind of function?
questions.explanation: His formula approximates sine as a ratio of quadratic expressions, a rational function.

questions.q: Bhaskara I's commentary is regarded as one of the earliest known prose works in Sanskrit on which subject?
questions.explanation: The Aryabhatiyabhasya is among the oldest surviving Sanskrit prose works dealing with mathematics and astronomy.

questions.q: In his commentary, Bhaskara I famously discussed and gave solutions related to which type of equation studied in number theory?
questions.explanation: He treated first-order indeterminate equations (the kuttaka method) for integer solutions.

questions.q: Which statement best describes the role of Aryabhata in Bhaskara I's career?
options: Aryabhata's work was the foundation Bhaskara I expounded and defended
questions.explanation: Bhaskara I built upon, explained, and defended Aryabhata's astronomical system.

questions.q: Bhaskara I shares his name with the later Bhaskara II. What separates the two?
options: About five centuries and different works
questions.explanation: Bhaskara I (7th century) and Bhaskara II (12th century) are different scholars separated by roughly 500 years.

questions.q: Bhaskara I's astronomy, following Aryabhata, supported which then-unusual idea about the Earth?
options: That the Earth is the only body in the universe
That the Earth rotates on its own axis
That the Sun orbits a fixed Earth daily because the sky spins
questions.explanation: Following Aryabhata, his school favored the idea that the apparent daily motion of the sky is due to the Earth's rotation.

questions.q: Bhaskara I's works deal extensively with computing which astronomical phenomena?
options: Planetary positions, eclipses and lunar visibility
questions.explanation: His treatises address calculating planetary longitudes, eclipses, and the visibility of the Moon.

questions.q: Why is Bhaskara I's 629 CE text historically important for the numeral system used worldwide today?
options: It is the earliest dated text clearly using the decimal place-value system with a zero symbol
It introduced Roman numerals to India
It rejected the use of zero
questions.explanation: It is the oldest known dated document unambiguously employing the Hindu decimal place-value notation with zero.

questions.q: The subtitle 'The Sine Approximator' for Bhaskara I refers to his work on what mathematical idea?
options: Approximating the values of the sine function
questions.explanation: He devised a famous, surprisingly accurate approximation for sine values.

questions.q: In Bhaskara I's sine approximation, sin(x) for an angle x (in degrees, 0 to 180) is approximated using which quantity in the formula?
questions.explanation: His formula uses the product x(180 - x), giving sin(x) approximately 4x(180-x) / (40500 - x(180-x)).

questions.q: Bhaskara I's sine approximation appears in which of his works?
questions.explanation: The famous sine approximation formula is stated in his Mahabhaskariya.

questions.q: Bhaskara I's birthplace is often associated with which region, sometimes giving him the epithet relating to Saurashtra/Asmaka?
options: Possibly the Saurashtra or Asmaka region of India
questions.explanation: He is thought to have been born in the Saurashtra/Asmaka area of India, though details are uncertain.

questions.q: The Sanskrit name 'Aryabhatiyabhasya' for Bhaskara I's 629 CE work literally indicates that it is what?
questions.explanation: 'Bhasya' means commentary, so the title denotes his commentary on Aryabhata's Aryabhatiya.

questions.q: A notable feature of Bhaskara I's mathematics is his use of word-numerals and verses, but his 629 commentary is especially valued because it also uses what?
options: Decimal place-value numerals with a circle for zero
questions.explanation: Alongside word-numerals, he wrote numbers in the decimal positional system using a circle for zero.

questions.q: Which contemporary 7th-century Indian mathematician, known for rules on zero and negative numbers, was a near-contemporary of Bhaskara I?
questions.explanation: Brahmagupta (born 598 CE) was a near-contemporary, and the two represented different schools of the era.

questions.q: How accurate is Bhaskara I's sine approximation compared with true sine values over its range?
options: It is off by a factor of ten
It agrees to within a couple of percent
It is only correct at 0 and 180 degrees
It gives values larger than one for all angles
questions.explanation: His formula is remarkably accurate, with a maximum relative error of under about two percent (and a maximum absolute error around 0.0016) across 0 to 180 degrees.

questions.q: Within the history of trigonometry, Bhaskara I's contribution is significant because it provided an early what?
options: Closed-form rational approximation of a trigonometric function
questions.explanation: He gave one of the earliest known closed-form rational approximations of the sine function.
